title: EIP-7848: On-chain upgrade signaling
description: Adds a mechanism for clients to signal willingness for a network upgrade by including a reference implementation hash in each block header. A network upgrade activates only if a sufficient number of blocks within a defined voting window specify they are running the new software, replacing off-chain upgrade coordination with on-chain consent.
author: William Entriken (@fulldecent)
laymanDescription: Lets validators vote on-chain for network upgrades by including a special code in blocks they produce. An upgrade only goes live if enough blocks signal support within a set time window, giving the community direct say over whether changes are adopted.

endUsers.description: No direct impact on end users; changes the internal process by which network upgrades are coordinated and activated.

appDevs.description: No impact on application developers; the proposal affects upgrade governance mechanics, not smart contract interfaces or execution semantics.

walletDevs.description: No direct impact; wallets do not interact with block header signaling fields or upgrade activation logic.

toolingInfra.description: Block explorers and monitoring tools would need to parse and display the new referenceImplementationHash field and track voting window progress.

layer2s.description: No direct impact on layer 2 protocols; the proposal changes how L1 upgrades are activated but does not alter rollup or bridge mechanics.

stakersNodes.description: Validators gain an explicit on-chain mechanism to signal upgrade readiness, requiring them to evaluate reference implementations before opting in.

clClients.description: Consensus clients must implement voting window logic, track approval counts across blocks, and conditionally activate new behaviors based on vote outcomes.

elClients.description: Execution clients must add a referenceImplementationHash field to the block header after extraData and validate it during block processing.

benefits: Enables transparent, on-chain coordination for network upgrades via block-level signaling.
Ensures upgrades activate only with demonstrated supermajority support from block producers.
Provides real-time, verifiable feedback on upgrade readiness during the voting window.
Replaces off-chain announcement-driven upgrades with consent-based on-chain governance.
tradeoffs: Adds a new required field to block headers, increasing header size and validation complexity.
Overlapping or competing upgrade proposals could fragment validator support and cause confusion.
Long voting windows may delay subsequent upgrade proposals from being considered.
